results.title: Graphical fidelity is ruining video games / fuzzy notepad
results.content: This sucks. The design serves the realistic aesthetic, not the player’s experience, and has to be awkwardly contorted to fit into the game. The thing I love about Doom’s “abstract” design is that it can serve the gameplay first and be fit to aesthetics second. Metroid Prime did an excellent job of this as well, and I’d even call its design abstract — it has a lot more detail than Doom, sure, but the rooms are more like handwaved concepts than slices of a space that would reasonably exist. (Who would fill a cave with powered doors, anyway? Who cares?) [...] Then I load Fallout 3, and it tries to look like the real world, and it does a big song and dance asking me for my stats “in-world”, and it tries to imply I can roam this world and do anything I want and forge my own destiny. Then I get into the game, and it turns out I can pretty much just shoot, pick from dialogue trees, and make the occasional hamfisted moral choice. The gameplay doesn’t live up to what the environment tried to promise. The controls don’t even live up to what the environment tried to promise. [...] Or it might be the same tendency I see in modding scenes: the instinct to obsessively control the player’s experience, to come up with a perfectly-crafted gameplay concept and then force the player to go through it exactly as it was conceived. Even Doom 4, from what I can see, has a shocking amount of “oh no the doors are locked, kill all the monsters to unlock them!” nonsense. Why do you feel the need to force the player to shoot the monsters? Isn’t that the whole point of the game? Either the player wants to do it and the railroading is pointless, or the player doesn’t want to do it and you’re making the game actively worse for them!

results.title: Visual fidelity vs framerate: What should you you prioritize?
results.content:
Smooth gameplay isn't just a matter of aesthetics; it significantly affects player performance and enjoyment. Games with low framerates may suffer from stuttering, screen tearing, and input delay, leading to frustration and disengagement from the experience. As gaming technology advances, players increasingly demand higher framerates to fully immerse themselves in virtual worlds and to gain the upper hand in multiplayer environments. Thus, the framerate is a critical aspect of modern gaming, shaping the way players interact with and perceive digital landscapes.

### Advancements in framerate [...] ## Is one better than the other?

### Context matters

The debate over which aspect — visual fidelity or framerate — is superior in gaming is quite subjective, influenced by individual preferences, gaming habits, and technical considerations. Some argue that visual fidelity is paramount, as it enhances immersion and elevates the artistic merits of gaming as a medium. In open-world games, such as The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, Cyberpunk 2077, and Red Dead Redemption 2, visual fidelity takes center stage, immersing players in vast, detailed landscapes teeming with life. The lush environments and intricate character models contribute to the sense of realism, inviting exploration and discovery. [...] In addition to enhancing the aesthetic appeal, visual fidelity plays a crucial role in storytelling and atmosphere. Detailed environments and lifelike character models contribute to the narrative experience, allowing players to emotionally connect with the game world and its inhabitants. From sprawling open worlds to linear narrative-driven adventures, visual fidelity sets the stage for memorable gaming experiences, elevating the medium to new artistic heights. Visual fidelity also encompasses the impact of display resolution in addition to the aforementioned in-game effects.

### How visual fidelity is improving

results.title: The Graphical Fidelity of Video Games & Animated Films
results.content:
Awards . Now, in the long-awaited 2020 FF7 Remake (FF7R), Cloud Strife looks like he could very well exist in real life in terms of graphics fidelity. The city of Midgard, where FF7R is set, could probably exist in some alternate steampunk universe. Despite all these advancements, video games still lack in graphical fidelity when compared to computer-animated films. In 1997, Pixar, the award-winning animation studio now owned by Disney, was already creating films such as A Bug’s Life, in which the characters looked miles better than the block of pixels that was supposed to resemble Cloud. Pixar has since continued to make graphical improvements, evident in their recent films such as Incredibles 2 and Coco. However, it must be noted that the gap in graphical quality is much smaller than it [...] ### Abstract:

Throughout history, movies and games have had different expectations of them. Movies are expected to have gorgeous visual effects, and graphics are expected to look picture perfect. On the other hand, games have always been expected to have smooth, consistent gameplay. Recent advancements in the development of real-time rendering techniques have opened the possibility of games catching up their movie counterparts in graphical quality.

### Introduction [...] onto a 2D space occurs. The rasterization stage is where the 2D triangles are then ‘converted’ to pixels. Finally, at the pixel-shader stage, the pixels are assigned colors based on data from their world and a variety of different lighting algorithms . One of the most basic lighting models is known as the Phong Reflection Model. To calculate the final look of an object, the lighting model only needs data from the object, the direction of the camera, and information about the light source . The immediate problem with this is that in a realistic world, lighting does not always  shine in a single direction. It can be reflected, refracted, absorbed, and more . Light rays can bounce, and rasterization has trouble handling the amount of calculations needed to follow all the light rays. There
